title: Your first variable
instruction:
In Chapter 1 you printed your name by typing it directly. Now let's store it in a variable instead.

Here is how you create a variable in Go:

    name := "Alex"

The := means create this variable and put this value in it. Think of it as an arrow pointing right: name gets "Alex".

Create a variable called name with your actual name in it. Then print it using fmt.Println(name).
hint: Write name := "YourName" on one line. On the next line write fmt.Println(name). Your name goes inside double quotes.

codeChecks.message: Create a name variable like this: name := "YourName"
Print the variable by writing: fmt.Println(name)
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: Add a second variable
instruction:
You're off to a great start! Let's add another variable.

The name variable is already created. Now create a city variable with the name of your city, then print both — name first, then city.

Two variables, two Println calls, two lines of output.
hint: city := "Chicago" goes on a new line after name. Then add fmt.Println(city) after fmt.Println(name).

codeChecks.message: Create a city variable like this: city := "Chicago"
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: Use a variable three times
instruction:
Here is the power of variables. The program already creates a greeting variable. Your job is to print it three times in a row — three separate Println calls, all using the same variable.

No copying the text. Just use greeting three times.
hint: Write fmt.Println(greeting) three times. Each call reads from the same variable.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO line with your actual code.

title: Update a variable
instruction:
Variables can change. That is literally what the word variable means.

When you first create it, you use :=
When you update it, you use just =

The program creates a mood variable set to "tired". Your job is to update it to "energized" after the first print, then print it again. You should see tired first, then energized.
hint: After the first fmt.Println(mood), write: mood = "energized"  — single = not :=  Then add another fmt.Println(mood) below that.

codeChecks.message: Update mood using a single = like this: mood = "energized"  — not :=
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: Three variables, all yours
instruction:
You are killing it! Let's go from scratch.

Create three variables:
- firstName with your first name
- lastName with your last name
- hometown with the city you are from

Then print all three, one per line.
hint: Three := lines, three Println lines. That is all you need.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.
